_provenance: Reference agent outputs generated with the system prompts in src/f1agents/agents/prompts/ against the payloads in batch_input.jsonl. Regenerate against your own AWS account with: python -m f1agents.pipeline.run_race_analysis --year 2024 --race Spanish --invoke
Spanish_Grand_Prix-stint-analyst: Three things matter. First, Norris is the tyre reference: 0.063 s/lap fuel-corrected decay against a field median of 0.069, on the fastest median clean pace (79.732s). Second, Perez is burning tyres at 0.096 s/lap, roughly 50% over Verstappen's 0.078; on a 22.6s pit-loss circuit that closes any offset window he had. Third, Russell's stint residual IQR of 1.436s is the tightest in the top six; his fitted slopes (0.084) are trustworthy, others carry wider bands. Fuel slope is -0.047 s/lap, so raw late-stint pace flatters everyone by about a second over a 20-lap stint; use the corrected slopes, not the screen deltas. Fits for Tsunoda are weak (CI spans 0.13-0.27), treat his numbers as indicative only.
Spanish_Grand_Prix-rival-watcher: Realized: the undercut is paying heavily today. Hamilton took 15.87s out of Piastri across the L16/L21 cycle (gap 3.19 -> 19.06) and 10.54s out of Leclerc at L43/L47. Perez gained 11.21s on Stroll from a four-lap offset. Measured swings of 10-16s against a 22.61s pit loss mean a rival pitting first from inside ~4s converts to track position almost every time. Largest open exposure: any car defending inside 4s with tyres older than five laps is losing the position if the rival commits first. Cover, or accept the offset and plan the overtake on pace delta.
Spanish_Grand_Prix-deg-explainer-0: Bottas stint 2 (L12-28) decays at 0.275 s/lap, z=4.79 versus the 0.069 field median, CI 0.204-0.313, so the signal is real, not fit noise. Ranked hypotheses: (1) compound-specific failure or graining on this set: his own stints 1 and 3 are normal (0.042, 0.059) and teammate Zhou's parallel stint 2 is 0.053, so the car is not the cause; confirmed by tyre compound and set history. (2) Progressive traffic exposure: residual IQR 0.521s is elevated but not extreme; confirmed by GPS gap-ahead density. (3) Damage picked up near L12: would need floor/aero telemetry. The L28 stop, earlier than the strategy around him, is consistent with the team seeing hypothesis 1 live.
Spanish_Grand_Prix-driver-coach-NOR: Strength: tyre usage. Fuel-corrected decay of 0.063 s/lap was the best among the lead group (Verstappen 0.078, Piastri 0.066) while holding the fastest median clean lap, 79.732s vs Verstappen's 79.805s. That combination is what made the long first stint viable at all. Cost: lap-to-lap spread. Consistency IQR of 2.439s against Verstappen's 2.022s and Russell's 1.436s; roughly 0.4s of in-stint variance conceded to the car ahead, which matters when the final gap was 2.22s. Focus: the variance is concentrated in the middle stint per the residuals; review L30-45 traffic management. Strategy note for the engineer, not the driver: the pit model priced the L23 stop at 4.38s versus an L17 stop.
Spanish_Grand_Prix-race-reporter:
Executive summary: Barcelona 2024 was decided in the pit windows, not on track. The undercut was worth 10-16s per cycle against a 22.6s pit loss. Verstappen's first stop was priced within 0.06s of the model optimum; Norris's was 4.38s off, and he finished 2.22s behind.

The strategy story. Barcelona ran clean (neutralisation rate 0.0008 across the era sample), which made it a pure tyre-offset race. Pit loss measured 22.61s (IQR 2.24s over 42 stops). Eighteen pit battles resolved in the data, and the undercut won nearly all of them: Hamilton's L16 stop swung 15.87s against Piastri's L21 response, Perez took 11.21s from Stroll on a four-lap offset, and Hamilton repeated the move on Leclerc late, 10.54s across L43/L47. With swings of that size, first-mover advantage dominated the middle phase.

At the front, the counterfactual model (fixed pit loss, linear per-stint deg, no traffic interaction) prices Verstappen's L17 first stop within 0.06s of his optimum. Norris, on the race's best tyre life (0.063 s/lap corrected), stayed out to L23; the same model says L17 was worth 4.38s of race time. He crossed the line 2.22s behind. Under the model's assumptions, the win was live at the first stop. The anomaly desk flagged Bottas's second stint at 0.275 s/lap decay (z=4.79, field median 0.069) with teammate and own-adjacent stints normal, consistent with a compound or set-specific problem and an early L28 stop.
Spanish_Grand_Prix-track-historian: Barcelona sits mid-field on tyre stress: median corrected decay 0.069 s/lap across 2022-2024 against a 25-circuit spread of 0.046-0.175. Its defining features are elsewhere: the lowest neutralisation rate in the sample (0.0008; effectively no SC/VSC influence) and 2.15 stops per driver, so strategy here is a deterministic offset game rather than an opportunism game. Nearest neighbours on the feature set: Red Bull Ring (deg 0.057, pit loss 21.8s) and Yas Marina (0.060, 24.0s). What transfers from those races: undercut-first priors, pit-loss sensitive window planning, low weighting on SC gambles. What does not: position volatility. Barcelona's 3.17 places of average grid-to-finish movement exceeds both neighbours, so track position is more recoverable on pace here; a deg offset is worth defending with, not just protecting.
Spanish_Grand_Prix-compliance-guardian-1: NON-COMPLIANT. SR-30.5m-two-compounds: the plan runs medium/medium; a dry race requires two distinct dry compounds. Smallest fix: make either stint soft or hard. GOV-advisory-only: auto_execute is set true; agent recommendations must remain advisory. Smallest fix: set auto_execute false and route through the race engineer. All other verdicts passed.
